disclaimer: FIXTURE_ID=transcript_sleep. This transcript is fictional. All content synthesized for evaluation purposes only and does not represent any real clinical encounter or patient.
full_text: Patient reported persistent insomnia over the past six weeks with difficulty falling asleep and frequent middle-of-night waking. Therapist introduced sleep hygiene guidelines including consistent wake times and a screen curfew, plus a brief CBT-I stimulus control framing. Patient agreed to track sleep for the week and implement a 10pm screen curfew.

segments.text: I haven't slept properly in six weeks. It takes me an hour to fall asleep and then I'm wide awake at two or three.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Six weeks is long enough that we should treat it seriously. What does your typical evening look like right now?
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: I work until maybe ten, watch TV in bed for another hour, scroll my phone when I can't sleep. Sometimes I just give up and work more.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Okay. Let's talk about sleep hygiene first — it sounds basic but the evidence is really strong. The two biggest levers are consistent wake time and a screen curfew.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Consistent wake time even on weekends?
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Yes. Especially on weekends. Within thirty minutes of the same time every day anchors your circadian rhythm.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: That's going to be hard. I sleep in on Saturdays.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: I know. And the screen curfew — ideally ninety minutes before bed, no screens. Tonight we can aim for a 10pm curfew and see how that lands.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: 10pm screen curfew. Okay. What do I do instead?
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Read a physical book. Take a warm shower. Low lights. The goal is signaling to your nervous system that sleep is coming.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: And for the middle-of-night waking?
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: This is from CBT-I, cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia. The rule is: if you're awake for more than twenty minutes, get out of bed. Go to another room. Read something boring in dim light. Only return to bed when sleepy.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Get out of bed? That feels counterintuitive.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: It is. But staying in bed awake teaches your brain that bed is a place for wakefulness. This is called stimulus control — we want bed to mean only sleep.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Huh. Okay. Stimulus control. I'll try the twenty-minute rule.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Would you be willing to track your sleep for the week? Just a quick log of bedtime, wake time, and any middle-of-night waking?
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Yeah, I can do a sleep log. I have an app for that already.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Perfect. So this week: consistent wake time, 10pm screen curfew, twenty-minute rule, sleep log. That's a lot, but they're all one-dial changes.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Noted. And if it's not helping after a week?
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Then we revisit. We might look at whether anxiety is driving the waking, which is a different conversation.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

test_queries.query: What sleep hygiene rules did we set up?
What do I do when I can't sleep in the middle of the night?
